data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider Akamai Technologies (AKAM) After Its Recent Share Price Jump?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Akamai Technologies is priced fairly or if there is still value on the table, this article walks through what the current share price may be implying.
  • The stock last closed at US$111.76, with reported returns of 17.5% over 7 days, 23.3% over 30 days, 31.3% year to date, 11.7% over 1 year, 44.6% over 3 years, and 13.0% over 5 years, which gives plenty for valuation focused investors to think about.
  • Alongside these share price moves, recent coverage around Akamai has centered on its position in software and content delivery and how investors are weighing that against broader sector trends. This provides useful context when you are asking whether the current price and expectations are in sync.
  • Simply Wall St currently gives Akamai a value score of 2 out of 6, reflecting that it screens as undervalued on 2 of 6 checks. Next we will walk through the main valuation approaches used here before coming back at the end to a more complete way to think about what that score really means.

Akamai Technologies scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Akamai Technologies Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes projected cash flows a company may generate in the future and discounts them back to today to estimate what the business could be worth right now.

For Akamai Technologies, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on cash flow projections. The latest twelve month free cash flow is reported at about $649.8 million. Analysts provide specific free cash flow estimates up to several years out, and Simply Wall St then extrapolates those further, with projections ranging from $899.3 million in 2026 to $472.3 million in 2035. Within that, the projection for 2030 is $620 million.

After discounting these projected cash flows, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $47.07 per share. Against the recent share price of US$111.76, this implies the stock is 137.4% overvalued based on this DCF framework.

Result: OVERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Akamai Technologies may be overvalued by 137.4%. Discover 53 high quality undervalued stocks or create your own screener to find better value opportunities.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Akamai Technologies.

Approach 2: Akamai Technologies Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Akamai Technologies, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to think about what you are paying for each dollar of earnings. Investors usually accept a higher P/E when they expect stronger growth or see lower risk, and a lower P/E when they expect slower growth or see higher risk.

Akamai’s current P/E is 31.7x, compared with the IT industry average of about 23.2x and a peer group average of 43.2x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Akamai is 31.8x, which is their estimate of what a “normal” P/E might look like after factoring in elements such as earnings growth profile, profit margins, industry, market cap and specific risks.

This Fair Ratio is designed to be more tailored than a simple comparison with peers or the broad industry, because it adjusts for company specific traits rather than assuming all IT stocks should trade on the same multiple. With Akamai’s actual P/E of 31.7x sitting very close to the Fair Ratio of 31.8x, the shares appear to be trading at roughly the level this framework would suggest.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

  • The company advanced its international strategy through new rig deployments and joint ventures in key markets.

Nabors Industries, traded on the NYSE under the ticker NBR, is a contract driller with a global presence across onshore and offshore markets. These recent moves come as the oilfield services sector continues to adjust to shifts in customer spending, regional activity patterns, and technology-driven efficiency gains. The acquisition, divestiture, and international expansion steps, together with debt reduction, represent a period of meaningful repositioning for Nabors Industries. The Parker Wellbore acquisition, Quail Tools sale, and US$554 million net debt reduction point to a business that is being refocused around higher value drilling and well services with a leaner balance sheet. Parker adds scale in regions like the Middle East and Latin America and broadens Nabors Industries' mix of rigs and wellbore services, which can matter when competing with peers such as Helmerich & Payne, Patterson UTI and Precision Drilling for multi year contracts. Exiting Quail Tools simplifies the portfolio and frees up capital, while lower net debt and about US$45 million less annual cash interest give the company more flexibility if conditions tighten.

  • The acquisition of Parker Wellbore feeds directly into the narrative around integration of acquired assets, international exposure and technology driven services, which are cited as key drivers for margins and cash generation.

  • The earnings swing from a full year net loss to a profit of US$286.62 million and the move to positive EPS are not fully reflected in the narrative framing that expects the company to remain unprofitable over the next few years.

data.title: Is It Time To Reassess CRH (CRH) After Its Recent Share Price Strength?
data.description:
• If you are looking at CRH and wondering whether its current share price really reflects what you are getting, this article walks through the key numbers that matter for value focused investors.
  • CRH last closed at US$125.97, with returns of 17.6% over 1 year, 3.3% over 30 days, a 0.9% decline over 7 days, and a 0.4% decline year to date. These figures can influence how the market is thinking about both its growth potential and risk.
  • Recent coverage around CRH has mainly centered on its position in the building materials sector and how investors are reacting to that backdrop. This helps frame these price moves, and this mix of short term pullbacks and longer term gains often gets investors asking whether the current valuation still stacks up.
  • Our Simply Wall St value score for CRH is 2 out of 6, which reflects how many of our valuation checks currently flag the shares as undervalued. Next we will look at the main valuation approaches behind that score before finishing with a more complete way to think about what the stock might be worth.

CRH scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: CRH Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes estimates of the cash CRH could generate in the future, then discounts those back to what they might be worth in today’s dollars. It is essentially asking what a stream of future cash flows is worth right now.

CRH’s latest twelve month Free Cash Flow is about US$3.12b. Using a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model, analysts and subsequent extrapolations project Free Cash Flow rising to US$4.78b by 2030, with intermediate years such as 2026 and 2027 sitting in the US$3.34b to US$3.74b range. Beyond the explicit analyst period, Simply Wall St extends the projections through to 2035 using modest incremental growth assumptions.

When all of these projected cash flows are discounted back and summed, the model arrives at an intrinsic value of roughly US$108.77 per share. Compared with the recent share price of US$125.97, the DCF implies CRH is about 15.8% overvalued on this set of assumptions.

Result: OVERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for CRH.

Approach 2: CRH Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like CRH, the P/E ratio is a useful way to link what you pay per share to the earnings the business is currently generating. It helps you see how many dollars investors are willing to pay today for each dollar of recent earnings.

What counts as a "normal" P/E really depends on how the market views a company’s growth prospects and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher risk tends to line up with a lower one.

Approach 1: Etsy Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes estimates of the cash a business could generate in the future and discounts those amounts back to what they might be worth today. It is a way of translating future cash flows into a single present value figure.

For Etsy, the model uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on cash flow projections. The latest twelve month free cash flow is reported at about $645.4 million. Analyst projections and subsequent extrapolations by Simply Wall St extend out to 2035, with free cash flow in 2030 projected at $826.1 million, all in $.

When those projected cash flows are discounted back, the DCF model produces an estimated intrinsic value of around $109.54 per share. Compared with the recent share price of about $45.73, this framework suggests Etsy trades at a discount of roughly 58.3%, which screens as undervalued on this measure alone.

Investors usually accept a higher or lower P/E depending on what they expect for future earnings growth and how risky they feel those earnings might be, so there is a wide range of what can count as a normal or fair P/E.

data.title: Atmus Expands Industrial Reach With Koch Filter Deal And New Board Voice
data.description:
• Atmus Filtration Technologies (NYSE:ATMU) completed the acquisition of Koch Filter Corporation, creating a new Industrial Solutions segment.
  • The company also appointed Heath Sharp to its Board of Directors, adding experience in growing international industrial businesses.

Atmus Filtration Technologies, known for filtration products across transportation and industrial applications, is broadening its reach with the Koch Filter acquisition. The move brings a new Industrial Solutions segment into the mix and signals a wider focus across industrial air filtration and related end markets. For you as an investor, it adds another piece to how NYSE:ATMU is positioning its business mix.

The addition of Heath Sharp to the Board of Directors introduces governance experience that is directly tied to scaling global industrial operations. The impact will depend on execution, and this combination of a new business segment and additional board expertise provides more information to consider when assessing Atmus's future footprint and risk profile.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Atmus Filtration Technologies by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Atmus Filtration Technologies.

We've flagged 1 risk for Atmus Filtration Technologies. See which could impact your investment.

This deal drops into an already busy period for Atmus. The company reported fourth quarter 2025 sales of US$446.6 million and net income of US$48 million, took impairment charges of US$8.4 million on long lived assets, issued 2026 guidance for revenue of roughly US$1.95b to US$2.02b, and affirmed a quarterly dividend of US$0.055 per share. Adding Koch Filter on top of that expands Atmus further into industrial air applications, an area that can behave differently to its transportation heavy aftermarket base. The new Industrial Solutions segment gives Atmus another lever for growth and mix, while the board appointment of Heath Sharp brings direct experience in turning a regional industrial business into a global one. For investors, the key question is how quickly Industrial Solutions contributes in a visible way to revenue, margins and cash flow, and whether integration costs or additional write downs appear as the Koch assets are embedded.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Atmus Filtration Technologies Narrative

  • The acquisition supports the narrative that Atmus is using targeted M&A to extend beyond transportation into broader industrial filtration, widening its addressable market and potentially supporting recurring earnings from more end markets.
  • The impairment charges and integration work that often come with acquisitions could challenge assumptions in the narrative about a smooth shift into higher margin areas if restructuring needs are larger or more prolonged.
  • The creation of a dedicated Industrial Solutions segment and the appointment of Heath Sharp add organizational and governance elements that are not fully reflected in the original storyline, especially around how Atmus might build a deal pipeline anchored on Koch Filter.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Atmus Filtration Technologies to help decide what it is worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Integration risk if Koch Filter requires more investment or restructuring than expected, particularly after the recent US$8.4 million impairment on long lived assets.
  • ⚠️ Balance sheet flexibility, given analysts have flagged debt levels as a risk, which can limit room for further deals if cash needs rise.
  • 🎁 Broader industrial exposure alongside transportation, which can smooth revenue across cycles and support recurring demand for filtration products.
  • 🎁 A board that now includes Heath Sharp, whose experience in scaling an industrial manufacturer internationally may strengthen oversight of M&A and operations.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, investors may want to track how Atmus discloses the performance of the new Industrial Solutions segment, including revenue contribution, margins and any one off integration costs tied to Koch Filter. The 2026 guidance range of US$1.945b to US$2.015b in revenue sets a reference point for how much the deal and any follow on M&A actually show up in the numbers. Board level changes also matter, so it can be useful to watch any shifts in capital allocation between acquisitions, share repurchases and dividends, and to monitor how management describes the industrial filtration pipeline on future earnings calls.
